California 
Fair Political 
Practices Commission 
Dave Maxwell, Treasurer 
1411 Donohue Drive 
San Jose, CA 
95131 
Dear Mr. Maxwell: 
May 5, 1989 
Re: 
Our File No. G-89-267 
Pursuant to your request, a copy of Elections Code sections 
12400-12407 is enclosed. 
These laws are not part of the Political 
Reform Act, therefore, the Commission cannot assist you concerning 
their interpretation. 
We suggest that you contact the Attorney 
General's office if you have questions about permitted uses of 
surplus funds. 
Sincerely,

Chapter 5. Limitations on Uses of Campaign Funds 
12401. 
more than two national committee members from California, chairman of the state 
central committee, executive committee of the state central committee, or executive 
committee of the county central committee of the party whose name is being used 
in the county in which the solicitation is to be made. If the county central commit-
tee of the party in that county does not have an executive conu::t1ittee, the written 
consent of the chairman and secretary of that county central committee is sufficient. 
All persons soliciting funds in accordance with this section shall be furnished 
with adequate credentiais bearing the name of the solicitor and a copy of the writ-
ten consent which bears the signature of the person authorizing the solicitation. 
(Amended by Stats. 1976, c. 224, §8.) 
12302. Authorization to use candidate or committee name. 
It is unlawful for any person who solicits funds for the purpose of supporting 
or promoting any candidates or committees to include in any part of its name the 
name of that candidate or committee unless that person shall have previously ob-
tained the authorization of the candidate or committee or the candidate's or 
committee's designated agent to use the candidate's or committee's name in the 
name of that person. 
Authorization by a candidate or committee shall not be construed as rendering 
the person soliciting funds a controHed committee as defined by Section 82016 of 
the Government Code. 
(Added by Stats. 1979. c. 872, §2.) 
12303. Notice of nonauthorization to be included in fundraising 
communication. 
Any person who solicits or receives contributions on behalf of any candidate or 
committee for the purported and exclusive use of that committee or the candidate's 
election campaign and who is not authorized by the candidate or committee or the 
candidate's or committee's designated agent to do so, shall include a notice in any 
fundraising communication, whether through any broadcasting station, 
newspaper, magazine, printed literature, direct mailing, or any other type of 
general public advertising, or through telephone or individual oral fundraising ap-
~a!, clearly and conspicuously stating that the person is not authorized by the can-
didate or committee and that the candidate or committee is not responsible for the 
of that person. 
(Added by Stats.1979, c. 872, §3.) 
Actualor~!!~~~~"""""""~""""""""""""~ 
The Legislature finds and declares that the political process is strengthened and 
confidence in elected officiais will be enhanced by limiting the uses of cam-
contributions. However, the making or receipt of a campaign contribution 
not create an actual or resulting trust or a trust-like relationship limiting the 
of campaign contributions. 
The Legislature further finds that any restrictions on the use of political funds 
bI; ~arefully drawn and narrowly construed to prevent any chilling effect on 
litica! 
and the freedom of elected officials to carry out legitimate 
'uunerltaI duties. 
by Stats.1981, c. 956, §1')

sections.question_synthetic: What is the proper interpretation and permissible use of surplus campaign funds and what procedures must be followed regarding their disposition?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC does not have authority to interpret Elections Code sections 12400-12407 regarding surplus campaign funds, as these laws are not part of the Political Reform Act. The requestor should contact the Attorney General's office for guidance on this matter.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document is a declination letter with no formal Q/C sections. Contains attachments with Elections Code provisions and general guidance on campaign fund limitations, but the core FPPC response is a jurisdictional declination. Some text quality issues due to document scanning/OCR.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: What is the proper interpretation and permissible use of surplus campaign funds and what procedures must be followed regarding their disposition?

CONCLUSION: The FPPC does not have authority to interpret Elections Code sections 12400-12407 regarding surplus campaign funds, as these laws are not part of the Political Reform Act. The requestor should contact the Attorney General's office for guidance on this matter.

embedding.summary: The FPPC responds to Dave Maxwell's inquiry regarding surplus campaign funds by declining jurisdiction, explaining that Elections Code sections 12400-12407 fall outside the Political Reform Act and directing him to the Attorney General's office for interpretation.
